html:
Textbook reference: f = 1
The three panels show the rotational optical-flow field for increasing focal length. The middle panel is adjustable.
⚠ Figure failed to load

elements: three side-by-side elongated rectangular image planes, proportioned and spaced to match the reference figure
each rectangle labeled below with its respective focal length: 'f=1/3', 'f=1', or 'f=3'
within each rectangle: a dense evenly spaced grid (at least 16x10) of 2D optical flow arrows
arrows calculated for Y-axis camera rotation and varying with focal length exactly as in the reference (fan and magnitude change across field), not mirrored nor trivially horizontal
arrows normalized and scaled so the variation in arrow lengths and angular spread across panels matches the source, especially the increase in vector length and divergence near edges for small f
subtle bounding boxes or outlines for each panel matching figure aspect ratios and spacing
axes marks/labels as in the original (no 'image x'/'image y'), use tick marks if present

interactions.label: Focal length (f)
interactions.teaches: Shows how increasing focal length compresses and straightens the motion field, transforming from a fanshaped pattern to uniform horizontal flow.

interactions.label: Rotation speed (W_Y)
interactions.teaches: Scales the length of all motion vectors proportionally, demonstrating that rotation speed alters magnitude but not field geometry or direction.

interactions.label: Show reference panels
interactions.teaches: Lets you toggle on/off the canonical f=1/3,1,3 panels beneath the main interactive panel for direct comparison to textbook cases.

demo_steps.title: Focal length: very small
demo_steps.narration: Let's start with a short focal length, f = 1/3. The motion field curves outward strongly, forming a pronounced fan. Arrows near the image edges have large vertical components and grow longer towards the corners. This pattern is highly non-uniform—very different from side-to-side camera movement—and is caused by the geometric effect of short focal length during rotation.

demo_steps.title: Focal length: moderate (f=1)
demo_steps.narration: Now with focal length set to f = 1, the fan effect is less pronounced. The arrows point more horizontally across most of the image, with reduced vertical flow at the edges, but there is still noticeable variation from center to periphery. This demonstrates how moderate focal lengths begin to regularize the motion field.

demo_steps.title: Focal length: large (f=3)
demo_steps.narration: With a long focal length, f = 3, the motion field becomes nearly uniform and entirely horizontal. The arrows are of similar length and direction everywhere, closely resembling the motion field from pure lateral translation. This highlights how at large f, the rotational motion field loses its distinguishing spatial variation.

demo_steps.title: Scaling only the rotation speed
demo_steps.narration: Now we'll double the rotation speed (W_Y), keeping focal length at f = 1. Notice how every arrow simply grows in length—none change their orientation or fan structure. This shows that increasing rotation speed only scales the magnitude of motion everywhere, without affecting the geometric pattern itself.

demo_steps.title: Explore: reference overlays off
demo_steps.narration: Try turning off the fixed reference panels to experiment freely with focal length and rotation speed in the main field. This isolates your adjustments and helps you visualize the continuous transition between classic textbook cases.

plan.camera_suggestion: Orthographic camera perpendicular to the image planes with enough zoom to show all three panels and reference overlays. Spacing and aspect ratio should match the elongated rectangles of the printed figure, filling the viewport horizontally. No perspective skew.
plan.notes: When calculating motion fields, compute the projected vector at each (x, y) so that low-f behaves like the original's highly divergent pattern, and high-f compresses vectors to nearly uniform horizontal. Normalize arrow lengths for each panel so relative magnitudes match textbook; use denser grids, especially near edges, to reflect full-field structure. Prefer SVG or high-res canvas for crisp arrows. Adjust axis labels or ticks to closely match the original's minimal style.

plan.contextChunk:
\end{bmatrix}
W_Y 
\end{aligned}$$ 

In this case, the focal length $f$ will have a strong
effect on the appearance of the motion field. For very large $f$, we can
approximate the 2D motion field by $\dot{x} \approx f W_Y$ and
$\dot{y} \approx 0$. The resulting motion field is approximately
constant across the entire image and looks like lateral translation
motion. For very small $f$, the motion field will be similar to the one
produced by a homography and it will be very different to a lateral
camera motion. The flows shown in @fig-motion_wy_f03_f1_f3 correspond
to $f=1/3$, $f=1$, and $f=3$.

![Motion flows corresponding to a rotation around the 
$Y$-axis. (left) $f = 1/3$. (middle) $f = 1$. (right) $f = 3$.](figures/optical_flow/camera_motion_field_rotation_three_f.png){width="100%" #fig-motion_wy_f03_f1_f3}

Camera rotation around the $Y$-axis does not inform about the scene
structure, but it informs about the camera parameters. The magnitude of
$W_Y$ only affects the scaling of the motion vectors, but it does not
change their orientation.

In the case of camera rotation, for a large angle (or a large
$\Delta t$), the relationship between the image at time $t$ and the
image at time $t+\Delta t$ is a homography.

### Motion under Varying Focal Length

Before moving into motion estimation, let's consider one final scenario:
a static camera observing a static scene, but the focal length changes
over time. What will the motion field be? In this setting, despite that

elements: 3 side-by-side rectangular image planes
each rectangle containing a grid of 2D motion vectors (arrows) representing optical flow
grid points in each rectangle arranged evenly
arrow directions and magnitudes varying based on position and focal length
labels under each rectangle: 'f=1/3', 'f=1', 'f=3'
bounding boxes for each rectangle

interactions.label: Focal length (f)
interactions.teaches: Shows how the focal length changes the pattern of motion vectors caused by Y-axis rotation.

interactions.label: Rotation (W_Y)
interactions.teaches: Alters the magnitude of the motion vectors without changing their directions, demonstrating how only the speed of rotation matters for motion magnitude.

demo_steps.title: Start: Small Focal Length
demo_steps.narration: With a small focal length of f = 1/3, the motion field caused by rotation around the Y-axis shows a pronounced divergence across the image. Notice how the arrows fan out, with strong vertical as well as horizontal components, especially near the corners. This pattern is very different from the case of pure lateral camera motion.

demo_steps.title: Middle: Moderate Focal Length
demo_steps.narration: Here, at f = 1, the motion field is less divergent. Most arrows are now more horizontally aligned, but there is still visible variation across the image, particularly at the edges. The vertical component is less pronounced compared to when f was small.

demo_steps.title: End: Large Focal Length Approximates Translation
demo_steps.narration: At a large focal length, such as f = 3, the motion field becomes nearly uniform and horizontal. The arrows are almost all the same direction and length, mimicking the appearance of lateral translation, even though the motion is due to camera rotation.

demo_steps.title: Scaling the Rotation Speed
demo_steps.narration: Now, let's increase the rotation speed (W_Y) while keeping the focal length fixed at f = 1. You can see that all the arrows grow proportionally in length, but keep exactly the same orientation as before. This shows that only the speed of rotation changes the size of the motion vectors—never their direction.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Orthographic top-down view facing the plane of the rectangles, with enough zoom to keep all three viewports visible side by side. No perspective distortion.
interactionPlan.notes: Keep vector arrow scaling consistent for visually meaningful comparisons. Use clear axis labels and overlay the focal length value. The slant of the arrows should be recalculated for each focalLength value in real time. For teaching impact, smoothly animate the transition between demo steps.

attempts.html:
Middle: Moderate Focal Length
Use the sliders to change the middle panel. The left and right panels remain fixed references.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: Arrow vector patterns in each panel do not fully match the original: the left and right panels have oversimplified arrow flows, especially lacking the strong fan and variation of directions near corners.
Arrows in the generated figure are more compact and centered than in the original, spanning a smaller portion of the panels.
Arrow density is lower; the arrow grid is coarser compared to the finer grid in the original.
Font, label style, and placement are similar but axis labels in the generated are less prominent and the 'image x'/'image y' labels differ from the original.
Panel aspect ratio and spacing are close but not as elongated or spread apart as in the original.
evaluation.notes: Engaging dynamics and step-through help learning, but the arrow field geometry and density do not accurately represent the structure or variation of the source panels, limiting fidelity and conceptual clarity.
action_items: Revise arrow calculations for left and right panels so vector field shapes and variation (fan and edge growth) match original; do not simply mirror the middle panel's formula.
Increase arrow grid density and ensure arrows expand closer to panel edges, matching the finer, more complete coverage of the reference.
Adjust panel proportions and spacing to better reflect the elongated rectangles and their spatial distribution in the source.
Update axis labeling to be more prominent and visually consistent with the original figure, possibly removing 'image x'/'image y' if not present.
Recalibrate vector normalization so relative arrow length and changes across the panels correspond visually with the original.

feedback.rationale: The 'Concept-Misunderstood' failure mode and action items about properly recalculating and differentiating vector fields indicate the plan failed to capture the correct conceptual decomposition, not just visual or parameter details.
action_items: Revise arrow calculations for left and right panels so vector field shapes and variation (fan and edge growth) match original; do not simply mirror the middle panel's formula.
Increase arrow grid density and ensure arrows expand closer to panel edges, matching the finer, more complete coverage of the reference.
Adjust panel proportions and spacing to better reflect the elongated rectangles and their spatial distribution in the source.
Update axis labeling to be more prominent and visually consistent with the original figure, possibly removing 'image x'/'image y' if not present.
Recalibrate vector normalization so relative arrow length and changes across the panels correspond visually with the original.

attempts.html:
Textbook reference: f = 1
The three panels show the rotational optical-flow field for increasing focal length. The middle panel is adjustable.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: All three panels match the vector field structure and border rectangles almost exactly.
Font and typesetting for the panel labels ('f = ...') and equation label are not a perfect match but are clear and in approximately correct style.
UI controls (sliders, narration, buttons) take up space on the left, partially overlapping the left-hand field.
The mathematical equation above the panels is present in the generated figure, but not in the original (though additive).
Panel framing/size, grid density, arrow styles, and spacing match the original closely; some minor alignment/size differences in the grid.
evaluation.notes: The figure superbly matches the original in structure and fields, with highly functional pedagogical interaction; minor visual/typographical/UI overlaps slightly reduce faithfulness.
action_items: Refine label font and typesetting to more closely match the original's math style (e.g., LaTeX rendering or improved italics placement).
Adjust UI positioning or add a 'hide UI' toggle to prevent UI overlap with the vector field panels for screenshots/displays.
Align the grid and arrow sizing a tiny bit more precisely to the original proportions if aiming for a perfect reproduction.
Optionally, remove or minimize the equation label above if strict faithfulness to the original is desired.

feedback.rationale: All discrepancies are minor visual or UI tweaks, with no conceptual or structural errors; the lack of failure modes and high scores indicate the figure is already highly faithful and usable.
